title: Discrete and Continuous Duality Algebras
speaker: Leonid Monin
abstract:
A classical construction associates a Poincare duality algebra to a homogeneous polynomial on a vector space. This construction was used to give a presentation for cohomology rings of complete smooth toric varieties by Khovanskii and Pukhlikov and of some spherical varieties (including full flag varieties) by Kaveh.
In my talk, I will recall the above results and will give two recent generalizations of the construction of duality algebras. The first one replaces the homogeneous polynomial by weighted homogeneous polynomial (and more general functions). In contrast to the classical construction,this allows us to construct Poincare duality algebras which are not necessarily generated in degree 1.  The second extension is the discrete analogue of the classical construction, which associates an algebra with Gorenstein duality to a polynomial on a lattice (free abelian group).  As a corollary, this provides a presentation for K-ring of smooth complete toric varieties as well as full flag varieties.

proof_stages.label: Classical Macaulay inverse-system construction
proof_stages.summary: Starting from a vector space V and a homogeneous polynomial f, the quotient of constant-coefficient differential operators by Ann(f) is a graded Poincare-duality algebra, and conversely every graded Poincare-duality algebra generated in degree one is obtained from its top-degree polynomial.

proof_stages.label: Volume-polynomial presentations of Chow rings
proof_stages.summary: For smooth complete toric varieties, divisors correspond to virtual polytopes whose volume computes top intersection degree, so the Chow ring is the differential-operator quotient by the annihilator of the volume polynomial, with parallel extensions to flag and spherical varieties.

proof_stages.label: Weighted homogeneous duality without degree-one generation
proof_stages.summary: The construction is generalized by allowing weighted homogeneous polynomials, and any graded Poincare-duality algebra generated in arbitrary degrees is recovered from a generating vector space via the polynomial x ↦ deg(exp(x)).

proof_stages.label: Toric variety bundles from a base generating polynomial
proof_stages.summary: Given a toric variety bundle and a polynomial presenting the even cohomology of the base, adding toric divisor classes and integrating the resulting base polynomial over the fiber polytope produces a weighted polynomial presenting the even cohomology of the total space.

proof_stages.label: Discrete inverse systems and Gorenstein duality
proof_stages.summary: Replacing vector spaces and differential operators by lattices and shift operators gives quotients of group algebras by annihilators of functions on the lattice, and these quotients satisfy an ungraded Gorenstein duality pairing.

proof_stages.label: K-theoretic realization by shift-operator annihilators
proof_stages.summary: The Euler-characteristic pairing makes K0 of smooth complete toric varieties Gorenstein, the discrete universality theorem applies to Gorenstein algebras generated by invertible elements, and K0 is described as shift operators on virtual polytopes modulo the annihilator of the lattice-point/Euler-characteristic function.

examples.name: The fan of projective plane and its triangle polytope
examples.note: For the toric surface P^2, the divisor aD1+bD2+cD3 is translated into the inequalities x≤a, y≤b, and -x-y≤c, making the divisor-to-polytope correspondence concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker draws the P2 fan, writes the ray generators and divisor coefficients, derives the inequalities x≤a, y≤b, −x−y≤c, and identifies the resulting triangle polytope

examples.name: Flag varieties and Gelfand-Zetlin polytopes
examples.note: Full flag varieties and more general spherical varieties are cited as further cases where divisor degrees are computed by volumes of polytopes, with Schubert classes represented via faces of Gelfand-Zetlin polytopes.
examples.engagement_reason: flag varieties and Gelfand-Zetlin polytopes are cited as another setting where the volume-polytope description and Schubert-class interpretations exist, without a concrete computation on a specific flag variety

passes.reason: Flag varieties with Gelfand-Zetlin polytopes form a systematic homogeneous family, used as another source of Chow-ring presentations.
